new_findings.title: Case definition change IgM→RT-PCR without sensitivity/specificity adjustment
new_findings.evidence: case definition: IgM seroconversion or RT-PCR positive per MINSAL circular B51/24
new_findings.mechanism: IgM-based diagnosis (pre-2012) has different sensitivity/specificity than RT-PCR (post-2012), potentially creating artificial temporal trends in case ascertainment independent of true incidence. No adjustment for diagnostic test performance applied.
new_findings.fix_hint: Conduct sensitivity analysis weighting pre/post-2012 cases by known IgM vs RT-PCR sensitivity/specificity ratios, or stratify walk-forward folds by diagnostic era
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Case definition IgM/RT-PCR documented in official panel metadata and manuscript v5, but no Q1 citation addressing sensitivity/specificity trade-offs of the 2024 diagnostic switch.

new_findings.title: Commune of probable infection assignment lacks validation
new_findings.evidence: assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
new_findings.mechanism: No validation data provided for infection-vs-residence assignment accuracy. If misclassification rate >13% (given 87% peridomestic claim), spatial mismatch between predictor (commune-level FSI) and outcome (mis-assigned cases) introduces non-differential exposure misclassification, biasing IRR toward null unpredictably.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report inter-rater reliability for infection commune assignment or conduct probabilistic sensitivity analysis across plausible misclassification rates (10-30%)
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Commune-of-infection assignment protocol mentioned in manuscript v5 and Supp Methods, but no validation statistics or Q1 citation quantifying misclassification rate.

new_findings.title: Rodent density proxy absent despite being causal mediator
new_findings.evidence: O. longicaudatus transmits the virus... subliminal two-to-fivefold rise in O. longicaudatus density — a phenomenon we term 'ratización'
new_findings.mechanism: FSI → rodent density → human cases is the stated causal path, yet no rodent abundance measurement appears in the model. Without this mediator, FSI coefficient conflates direct effects with unmeasured rodent population dynamics, potentially invalidating the ecological interpretation of ψ_R5.
new_findings.fix_hint: Add historical trapping data as sensitivity check, or explicitly state FSI is an instrumental variable with assumptions required for valid inference
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Ratización concept and O. longicaudatus causal role documented in hypothesis files and Estado_Conocimiento S50, but no direct rodent density covariate in final GLMM model.

new_findings.title: Wildfire veto threshold (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) lacks empirical justification
new_findings.evidence: active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination
new_findings.mechanism: Arbitrary threshold without validation against known fire perimeters. If threshold is too lenient, post-fire vegetation recovery (which may attract rodents) is misclassified as forest stress; if too strict, legitimate stress signals are censored, biasing FSI and attenuating ψ_R5 unpredictably.
new_findings.fix_hint: Validate threshold against CONAF fire perimeter database or conduct sensitivity analysis across ΔNBR2 ∈ [−0.10, −0.20]
new_findings.mcc_rationale: dNBR2 < -0.15 threshold implemented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion S51 scripts, but no peer-reviewed citation or empirical validation justifying -0.15 cutpoint for bamboo ecosystems.

new_findings.title: Negative ψ_R5 interpretation reversal unreported in pre-specification
new_findings.evidence: The negative ψ reflects R as a post-flowering scar signal: high R means the C. quila cycle has already peaked
new_findings.mechanism: Pre-specification likely assumed positive association (high stress → high cases). Post-hoc reinterpretation of negative coefficient as 'post-flowering scar' without documented a priori hypothesis risks narrative HARKing. If pre-spec v1.0 stated positive direction, sign reversal should trigger sensitivity checks.
new_findings.fix_hint: Publish pre-specification v1.0 directional hypothesis for ψ_R5; if reversed, report as exploratory finding requiring external validation
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Negative ψ_R5 interpretation present in decision_opcionA S50 and Paper_EID_Final, but pre-specification doc lacks explicit HARKing disclosure of sign reversal discovery.

new_findings.title: Companion clinical study selective outcome reporting risk
new_findings.evidence: A companion clinical analysis... has been submitted separately... share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics
new_findings.mechanism: If companion study failed to find prognostic associations, selective non-publication of null results while publishing positive ecological findings inflates apparent hantavirus predictability literature-wide. No trial registration or pre-specification mentioned for clinical study.
new_findings.fix_hint: Register companion clinical study retrospectively at ClinicalTrials.gov or state explicitly if negative findings will be published regardless of significance
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Dual-publication strategy documented in estrategia_dual S42, independence sentence in v5 manuscript, and Parte II master file confirms zero overlap. PREPRINT status mitigates outcome-reporting risk.

new_findings.title: 87% peridomestic transmission citation (Riquelme 2015) location mismatch
new_findings.evidence: 87% peridomestic transmission per Riquelme 2015 supports spatial accuracy
new_findings.mechanism: If Riquelme 2015 study was conducted outside Ñuble region or in different ecological zone, applying its 87% estimate to justify Ñuble commune-level assignment creates cross-population transportability error. Peridomestic fraction may differ in quiloide vs non-quiloide zones.
new_findings.fix_hint: Verify Riquelme 2015 study region; if non-Ñuble, conduct local chart review subsample to estimate Ñuble-specific peridomestic fraction
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Riquelme 2015 87% peridomestic figure cited in Auditoria_Q1_S50 and Paper_EID_Final, but no explicit geographic concordance check between Riquelme cohort and Ñuble study area.

new_findings.title: FSI composite formula weights frozen in Achibueno without Ñuble validation
new_findings.evidence: custom Forest Stress Index (FSI) derived from... NDMI/NDVI/NBR2 bands... calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble
new_findings.mechanism: Achibueno and Ñuble differ in elevation, forest composition, and precipitation regime. FSI weights optimized for Achibueno may mis-weight spectral bands in Ñuble's quiloide patches, introducing systematic measurement error in the primary exposure variable.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report sensitivity analysis with equal-weight FSI or validate current weights against field-sampled Ñuble C. quila patches
new_findings.mcc_rationale: FSI Achibueno calibration documented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion S51 and Estado_Conocimiento S50, but no Ñuble-specific validation analysis quantifying out-of-sample prediction error.

new_findings.title: CONAF seasonal patrol coverage (Oct-Apr) incomplete over risk window
new_findings.evidence: CONAF patrol coverage is seasonal (October–April), only partially overlapping the primary risk window
new_findings.mechanism: If CONAF detects quiloide flowering only during Oct-Apr, but cases occur year-round (including lag-5 from May-Sep flowering), off-season flowering events are systematically under-detected in FSI, creating seasonally varying exposure measurement error that confounds the lag-5 estimate.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report monthly FSI availability completeness or restrict analysis to Oct-Apr detection windows with explicit seasonal generalizability caveat
new_findings.mcc_rationale: CONAF seasonal Oct-Apr coverage explicitly stated in Auditoria_Q1_S50 cierre and Paper_EID_Final Methods. Temporal mismatch acknowledged as limitation in manuscript Discussion section.

new_findings.title: Zero September cases structural assumption untested
new_findings.evidence: 91.2% occurred October–May, with zero cases in September across 23 consecutive years
new_findings.mechanism: 23-year zero count in September likely reflects surveillance artifact (end-of-winter reporting lag) or true biological null period. If artifact, model's season_f factor absorbs spurious September effect; if biological, treating as fixed structural zero ignores rare-event tail risk (e.g., climate-shifted phenology).
new_findings.fix_hint: Test whether September zero-inflation is surveillance artifact via case-onset-vs-report-date analysis, or model as overdispersed Poisson rather than structural zero
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Zero September cases across 23 years documented in Panel Oficial M1M2 and Estado_Conocimiento S50, but no formal statistical test ruling out random chance or structural break.

new_findings.title: INE intercensal log-linear interpolation ignores migration shocks
new_findings.evidence: INE population estimates with log-linear intercensal interpolation
new_findings.mechanism: Log-linear interpolation assumes smooth exponential growth between censuses, missing abrupt migration events (e.g., 2010 earthquake, 2017 Ñuble administrative split). If population denominators are mis-estimated during shock years, incidence rate standardization introduces time-varying bias.
new_findings.fix_hint: Use INE's official annual projections if available, or flag census years as potential structural break points in sensitivity analysis
new_findings.mcc_rationale: No prior evidence found in memory/, obsidian_vault/, or audit/ regarding INE log-linear interpolation methodology or migration shock limitations. Genuinely new methodological critique.

new_findings.title: Forest cover fraction static despite 22-year study period
new_findings.evidence: static covariates (forest cover fraction, elevation, slope)
new_findings.mechanism: Forest cover changed 2002-2024 due to fires, logging, and CONAF reforestation. Using time-invariant baseline forest cover misses temporal confounding: communes with increasing cover may have rising rodent habitat independently of FSI, biasing spatial between-commune effects.
new_findings.fix_hint: Derive annual forest cover from Landsat time series or include linear forest-cover trend as sensitivity covariate
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior refs confirm static covariates (forest, elevation, slope) are documented in methods across multiple files, but no Q1 citation justifying 22-year static assumption nor numeric sensitivity analysis.

new_findings.title: Companion clinical manuscript creates selective outcome reporting risk
new_findings.evidence: A companion clinical analysis of 34 HCPS patients treated at Hospital Clínico Herminda Martín, Chillán, 2010–2025 has been submitted separately...share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics; only publicly available regional descriptors (total case count, case-fatality ratio, ethics approval) are common
new_findings.mechanism: Separate submission of clinical (n=34, 2010-2025) and ecological (n=136, 2002-2024) analyses from overlapping case populations raises selective reporting concern. If clinical manuscript reports null or contradictory findings for temporal patterns, readers lack cross-validation. Disclosure states 'no shared metrics' but does not address overlapping case inclusion or outcome definitions.
new_findings.fix_hint: Provide cross-tabulation of case overlap (how many of 34 clinical cases appear in 136 ecological cases); pre-register companion analysis jointly or explain why clinical temporal patterns were not incorporated as ecological predictors.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior evidence in project_estrategia_dual_publication_S42.md documents dual-publication strategy, separation of clinical/eco-epi manuscripts, and selective reporting mitigation already integrated into Methods.

new_findings.title: FSI lag-5 structural missingness (105 obs, 1.8%) creates unbalanced panel
new_findings.evidence: FSI lag-5 was structurally unavailable for the first five months of each commune's series (105 observations, 1.8%, excluded)
new_findings.mechanism: First 5 months of each commune (21 communes × 5 months = 105 obs) systematically excluded from analysis. If early-year observations differ systematically (e.g., seasonal transition effects, New Year administrative patterns), listwise deletion creates temporal imbalance. Small fraction (1.8%) limits material impact but could interact with September structural zero.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report whether excluded 105 observations include any non-zero cases; if yes, conduct sensitivity analysis imputing lag-5 FSI via seasonal climatology to retain temporal balance.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: FSI lag-5 structural missingness (105 obs, 1.8%) documented in project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md with explicit panel-balancing strategy and Methods sentence already integrated.

new_findings.title: Case-fatality ratio 27.9% computed on same 136 cases used for forecasting
new_findings.mechanism: Case-fatality is outcome-conditional statistic (deaths|cases) computed on same cases used for incidence forecasting. If high-CFR cases cluster temporally or spatially due to shared risk factors (e.g., delayed treatment in remote communes), CFR estimate is not independent of forecasting covariates. Minor concern given CFR reported as descriptive context, not model input.
new_findings.fix_hint: Stratify CFR by high-FSI vs low-FSI commune-months to test whether environmental risk predicts not just incidence but also severity; if no association, reinforces independence assumption.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: CFR 27.9% (38/136, Wilson CI 21.1–36.0%) computed in project_auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre_completo.md, cited in Estado_Conocimiento_Serie_Ecologica_S50.md, and integrated into Results per feedback_ci_obligatorio_Nbajo.md.

new_objections.title: Temporal leakage in case assignment protocol not described in Methods
new_objections.critique: Methods 5.5.1 states cases were 'assigned to their probable commune of infection...following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods' but provides zero detail. Retrospective assignment of cases to geographic units creates unmeasured temporal leakage risk—assignment may have used information unavailable at prediction time. This is fatal for walk-forward validation claims.
new_objections.suggested_response: Move reconciliation protocol from Supplement to main Methods with explicit listing of temporal cutoffs and data sources used. Demonstrate that assignment criteria (symptom onset location, residence history, travel records) were available ≥5 months before model prediction time for every reassigned case, or exclude reassigned cases in sensitivity analysis.

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 cited but unmeasured confounding structure ignored
new_objections.critique: Figure 2 annotation and prior objection R2-02 cite E-value=2.07 as weak. Worse: manuscript presents causal language ('ecological EWS', 'operational risk window', DAG in prior sessions) but provides zero quantitative bias analysis for: (i) rodent trapping effort varying with prior outbreak publicity, (ii) healthcare-seeking conditional on media coverage, (iii) Landsat overpass frequency correlating with cloud cover and case seasonality.
new_objections.suggested_response: Either remove all causal framing and present as associational prediction model, OR conduct formal quantitative bias analysis (array of bias parameters per VanderWeele 2019) for the three named confounders with realistic bounds from Chilean hantavirus literature. E-value alone is insufficient—need multi-confounder joint bias factors.

new_objections.title: Calibration assessment absent despite probabilistic forecast claims
new_objections.critique: Manuscript claims 'probabilistic EWS' and reports discrimination (AUC) and overall accuracy (log score, Brier) but never assesses calibration—the match between predicted probabilities and observed frequencies. For rare events, models can achieve good Brier/log scores while being systematically miscalibrated. No calibration plot, Hosmer-Lemeshow test, or calibration slope reported.
new_objections.suggested_response: Add calibration belt plot (Nattino et al. 2016 Stat Med) or logistic calibration slope/intercept with 95% CI from held-out folds. If calibration slope ≠1.0 or intercept ≠0, recalibrate predictions via Platt scaling before reporting decision curves. Acknowledge limitation if sample size prohibits stable calibration assessment at Tier 2/3.

new_objections.title: Bootstrap CI methodology switches between percentile and BCa without justification
new_objections.critique: Methods 5.5.5 specifies 'block bootstrap...with percentile 95% CI' for log score, but 5.5.7 specifies 'BCa bootstrap 95% CI' for AUC. Prior objection R1-01 flagged BSS percentile vs BCa inconsistency. No power/coverage simulation justifies when each method is appropriate. BCa requires more resamples for stable intervals; B=1000 may be insufficient (Efron rec: ≥2000 for BCa).
new_objections.suggested_response: Standardize on single CI method (recommend BCa with B=2000 throughout, citing Efron 1987 + Carpenter & Bithell 2000 already in text) OR provide simulation showing percentile CI achieves nominal 95% coverage for log score under your block structure. Report actual CI coverage from nested bootstrap if feasible.

new_objections.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] still lacks operational anchor
new_objections.critique: Prior objection R2-04 flagged lack of clinical/operational justification for DCA range. Methods 5.5.8 now states 'operational range p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20] annotated' but provides zero derivation. What is the cost ratio (false positive vs false negative) for CONAF patrol deployment or SEREMI education campaign? DCA without grounded harm/benefit weights is un-interpretable theatre.
new_objections.suggested_response: Derive threshold range from explicit operational costs: e.g., 'CONAF patrol costs ~X USD per commune-month; expected case hospitalization cost ~Y USD; ratio Y/X implies threshold p_t = 1/(1+Y/X) = Z.' If costs unavailable, present DCA as exploratory sensitivity analysis across plausible cost ratios, not as a decision-ready tool.

new_candidates.title: Learning Debt and Cost-Sensitive Bayesian Retraining: A Forecasting Operations Framework
new_candidates.abstract: Forecasters often choose retraining schedules by convention rather than by an explicit decision rule. This paper gives that decision a posterior-space language. We define learning debt as the divergence between the deployed and continuously updated posteriors, define actionable staleness as the policy-relevant latent state, and derive a one-step Bayes retraining rule under an excess-loss formulation. In an online conjugate simulation using the exact Kullback-Leibler divergence between deployed and shadow normal-inverse-gamma posteriors, a debt-filter beats a default 10-period calendar baseline in 15 of 24 abrupt-shift cells, all 24 gradual-drift cells, and 17 of 24 variance-shift cells, and remains below the best fixed cadence in a grid of cadences (5, 10, 20, and 40 periods) in 10, 24, an
new_candidates.reason: Bayesian retraining framework under excess-loss formulation, conceptually relevant to adaptive early warning update rules

new_candidates.title: Spatiotemporal dynamics of leptospirosis in Europe: a retrospective observational study with prospective projections
new_candidates.abstract: Background: Leptospirosis is a widespread zoonotic disease caused by Leptospira spp., with a substantial burden on both human and animal health. Transmission occurs through contact with the urine of infected animals, with rodents serving as the main disease reservoir. Climate and environmental changes, including higher temperatures, heavy rainfall, urbanization, forest encroachment, and biodiversity loss are expected to alter rodent populations and disease dynamics, raising concerns about the increasing risk of leptospirosis in Europe. Despite this, no comprehensive risk map or modelling study has been conducted at the European scale. Given this, we aimed to quantify spatiotemporal patterns of leptospirosis risk in Europe and project future incidence rate under climate change scenarios. Me
new_candidates.reason: Rodent-borne zoonosis in Europe with climate-driven risk modeling; direct parallel to Andes HCPS methodology

new_candidates.title: An informed monotone neural network for multi-stage chronic kidney disease prediction with integrated GFR estimation
new_candidates.abstract: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is a progressive condition affecting over 850 million people worldwide, where timely detection and accurate staging are critical to reducing morbidity, mortality, and healthcare burden. Current machine learning approaches often treat CKD prediction as a binary task, neglecting the ordered nature of disease stages, and rarely incorporate physiological constraints or calibrated probability estimates essential for clinical decision support. We propose a multi-stage CKD prediction framework that integrates ordinal classification, probability calibration, and a serum creatinine-monotonicity constraint within a knowledge distillation paradigm. A calibrated CatBoost model serves as a teacher, transferring temperature-scaled class probabilities to an ordinal neural net
new_candidates.reason: Multi-stage disease prediction with probability calibration; extends our Brier/RPS scoring framework conceptually

new_candidates.title: Benchmarking Q fever transmission in czech republic and serbia: A one health sub-national population study
new_candidates.abstract: Q fever, caused by Coxiella burnetii, poses a zoonotic threat worldwide. Understanding its epidemiology in diverse settings is crucial for effective control measures. A retrospective observational study compared Q fever epidemiology in the regions of Moravia and Silesia (Czech Republic) and Srem and South Bačka districts of the Autonomous Province of Vojvodina (Serbia), from 2011 to 2018. Here, we analyzed the demographic and spatial patterns of human and animal Q fever retrieved from the human and veterinary official surveillance databases. Animal seroprevalence of Coxiella burnetii was broad but consistently high in Czech cattle (range 8.6–60.0%) and was highest in Žďár nad Sázavou, Brno-město, and Znojmo districts. Moravia and Silesia saw an increasing average Q fever seroprevalence in
new_candidates.reason: Zoonotic disease surveillance with spatial-temporal analysis and retrospective observational design mirrors our epidemiological approach

new_candidates.title: Climate-driven changes in zoonotic risk of arenaviral hemorrhagic fevers in South America
new_candidates.abstract: Climate change is expected to significantly alter the ecological dynamics of zoonotic diseases, yet its long-term impact on rodent-borne hemorrhagic fevers in South America remains poorly understood. Here, we developed a robust predictive modeling framework that integrates species distribution models with a mechanistic force-of-infection approach to evaluate the effects of climate change on zoonotic risk of New World Arenaviruses. Using climate projections under Shared Socioeconomic Pathways SSP2-4.5 (moderate) and SSP5-8.5 (severe), our models predict a substantial increase in spillover risk across endemic and non-endemic regions over the next two decades. Projected increases in spillover risk for Guanarito, Machupo, and Junin viruses are primarily driven by climate-induced shifts in temp
new_candidates.reason: Climate-driven rodent-borne viral spillover models with lagged environmental predictors, directly comparable framework

new_candidates.title: VOLTA: The Surprising Ineffectiveness of Auxiliary Losses for Calibrated Deep Learning
new_candidates.abstract: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for deploying deep learning models in safety critical applications, yet no consensus exists on which UQ method performs best across different data modalities and distribution shifts. This paper presents a comprehensive benchmark of ten widely used UQ baselines including MC Dropout, SWAG, ensemble methods, temperature scaling, energy based OOD, Mahalanobis, hyperbolic classifiers, ENN, Taylor Sensus, and split conformal prediction against a simplified yet highly effective variant of VOLTA that retains only a deep encoder, learnable prototypes, cross entropy loss, and post hoc temperature scaling. We evaluate all methods on CIFAR 10 (in distribution), CIFAR 100, SVHN, uniform noise (out of distribution), CIFAR 10 C (corruptions), and Tiny ImageNet
new_candidates.reason: Comprehensive uncertainty quantification benchmark including temperature scaling and calibration methods
